perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is to be governed and construed according to the laws of the State of New York without regard to conflicts of law. The parties agree that each of them hereby submits to the jurisdiction of the New York State and federal courts for the purpose of resolving any dispute arising under this Agreement and that the exclusive venue for resolution of such disputes shall be state or federal courts located in Monroe County, New York.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is to be governed and construed according to the laws of the State of New York without regard to conflicts of law.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the jurisdiction and venue clause from the 'Governing Law, Jurisdiction and Venue' section and not placing it elsewhere, the contract becomes ambiguous regarding where disputes must be resolved.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 503
perturbation.law_explanation: CPLR § 503 outlines the proper venue for actions in New York. By omitting a venue clause, the contract fails to clearly establish where disputes should be litigated, potentially leading to litigation in improper or inconvenient venues, conflicting with the intent of the CPLR to provide clear venue rules.

Venue based on residence. (a) Generally. Except where otherwise prescribed by law, the place of trial shall be in the county in which one of the parties resided when it was commenced; the county in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred; or, if none of the parties then resided in the state, in any county designated by the plaintiff. A party resident in more than one county shall be deemed a resident of each such county. (b) Executor, administrator, trustee, committee, conservator, general or testamentary guardian, or receiver. An executor, administrator, trustee, committee, conservator, general or testamentary guardian, or receiver shall be deemed a resident of the county of his appointment as well as the county in which he actually resides. (c) Corporation. A domestic corporation, or a foreign corporation authorized to transact business in the state, shall be deemed a resident of the county in which its principal office is located; except that such a corporation, if a railroad or other common carrier, shall also be deemed a resident of the county where the cause of action arose. (d) Unincorporated association, partnership, or individually-owned business. A president or treasurer of an unincorporated association, suing or being sued on behalf of the association, shall be deemed a resident of any county in which the association has its principal office, as well as the county in which he actually resides. A partnership or an individually-owned business shall be deemed a resident of any county in which it has its principal office, as well as the county in which the partner or individual owner suing or being sued actually resides. (e) Assignee. In an action for a sum of money only, brought by an assignee other than an assignee for the benefit of creditors or a holder in due course of a negotiable instrument, the assignee's residence shall
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the jurisdiction and venue clause, which specifies where disputes arising from the agreement must be resolved. This omission contradicts CPLR § 503, which outlines the proper venue for actions in New York, as it fails to clearly establish the venue for disputes. The original text provided clarity by stating the exclusive venue was state or federal courts located in Monroe County, New York. Without this clause, the contract is ambiguous, and litigation could occur in improper venues, conflicting with the purpose of CPLR § 503 to provide clear venue rules, as explained in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, NEITHER PARTY SHALL BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER FOR ANY CONSEQUENTIAL, EXEMPLARY, SPECIAL, INCIDENTAL OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES, EVEN IF IT HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES, WHICH ARE RELATED TO THE AGREEMENT OR ITS BREACH.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, NEITHER PARTY SHALL BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER FOR ANY CONSEQUENTIAL, EXEMPLARY, SPECIAL, INCIDENTAL OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES, EVEN IF IT HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES, WHICH ARE RELATED TO THE AGREEMENT OR ITS BREACH. Preparation, maintenance and running of one (1) T-1 C5 Corvette race vehicle to race in:
perturbation.explanation: Moving the liability clause to after a description of vehicle, and making it follow after a description, makes it less apparent.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GOL § 5-326 addresses agreements exempting pools, gymnasiums, places of amusement or recreation from liability for negligence. While this contract isn't directly about those, the poorly placed exculpatory clause, mixed within unrelated text, undermines its enforceability. NY courts look for clear and unmistakable language when enforcing exculpatory clauses. By burying the liability disclaimer in an unrelated section, the modified text makes the exculpatory clause less obvious and thus more vulnerable to challenge.

General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 3 § 5-326. Agreements exempting pools, gymnasiums, places of public amusement or recreation and similar establishments from liability for negligence void and unenforceable. Every covenant, agreement or understanding in or in connection with, or collateral to, any contract, membership application, ticket of admission or similar writing, entered into between the owner or operator of any pool, gymnasium, place of amusement or recreation, or similar establishment and the user of such facilities, pursuant to which such owner or operator receives a fee or other compensation for the use of such facilities, which exempts the said owner or operator from liability for damages caused by or resulting from the negligence of the owner, operator or person in charge of such establishment, or their agents, servants or employees, shall be deemed to be void as against public policy and wholly unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The perturbation is a structural flaw that creates a legal contradiction. The original contract clearly states a liability clause. The changed text buries the original clause within irrelevant details regarding vehicle maintenance. The Law Explanation discusses NY GOL § 5-326 and how courts require clear and unmistakable language for exculpatory clauses. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing NY GOL § 5-326, states agreements exempting certain facilities from negligence are void and unenforceable. While the original contract clause is not directly about the facilities mentioned in the snippet, the principle of clarity for exculpatory clauses applies. By placing the clause amidst unrelated text, the change obscures the liability disclaimer, potentially making it unenforceable and thus creating a legal contradiction, consistent with the definition of Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Vendor represents and warrants to Torvec the following: (i) it has the authority to enter into this Agreement and to perform hereunder in accordance with its provisions; (ii) no other person or entity has the right to be the exclusive automotive sponsor of the activities and the events set forth in this Agreement; and (iii) it will perform its obligations under this Agreement in compliance with all applicable laws, rules and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor represents and warrants to Torvec the following: (i) it has the authority to enter into this Agreement and to perform hereunder in accordance with its provisions; (ii) no other person or entity has the right to be the exclusive automotive sponsor of the activities and the events set forth in this Agreement; and (iii) it will perform its obligations under this Agreement in compliance with all applicable laws, rules and regulations. 10. Notices All notices provided herein shall be in writing and are effective upon receipt if hand delivered, sent by overnight courier (with ability to confirm receipt), by fax or by registered or certified mail, return receipt requested, addressed to the respective parties hereto as follows:
perturbation.explanation: Moving the vendor's representation clause into the 'Notices' section obscures its importance. Representations and warranties are critical to contract formation and should be prominently displayed.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-312 implies warranties of title and non-infringement in sales contracts. While not a direct sale, the sponsorship agreement has elements where these warranties are crucial (e.g., vendor ensuring no conflicting sponsorships). Placing the representation about compliance with laws within the 'Notices' section diminishes its prominence, making it harder to argue it serves as an explicit warranty. The UCC requires clear language for warranty disclaimers/modifications, and hiding a relevant representation weakens its effect.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-312. Warranty of Title and Against Infringement; Buyer's Obligation Against Infringement. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-312. Warranty of Title and Against Infringement; Buyer's Obligation Against Infringement. (1) Subject to subsection (2) there is in a contract for sale a warranty by the seller that (a) the title conveyed shall be good, and its transfer rightful; and (b) the goods shall be delivered free from any security interest or other lien or encumbrance of which the buyer at the time of contracting has no knowledge. (2) A warranty under subsection (1) will be excluded or modified only by specific language or by circumstances which give the buyer reason to know that the person selling does not claim title in himself or that he is purporting to sell only such right or title as he or a third person may have. (3) Unless otherwise agreed a seller who is a merchant regularly dealing in goods of the kind warrants that the goods shall be delivered free of the rightful claim of any third person by way of infringement or the like but a buyer who furnishes specifications to the seller must hold the seller harmless against any such claim which arises out of compliance with the specifications. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original text clearly presents vendor representations and warranties. The changed text buries one of these warranties—compliance with laws—within the 'Notices' section, diminishing its prominence and potentially weakening its enforceability. This undermines the UCC's requirement for clear language regarding warranties, as stated in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers specifically to warranties of title and infringement under UCC § 2-312, the Law Explanation extends this principle to the sponsorship agreement, arguing that similar warranties are crucial in this context. By obscuring the representation about compliance with laws, the modified contract creates a structural flaw that weakens the warranty and introduces a legal contradiction as defined by the perturbation type.
